The Gaussian Moat Problem

A visual walkthrough: from prime lattice to transfer matrices, strip sampling, and Lyapunov exponents. Ten plots, zero JavaScript.

01 Gaussian Primes: The Lattice

Gaussian integers are complex numbers a+bi where a,b are integers. A Gaussian integer is prime if it can't be factored further (up to units). The open question: starting from any Gaussian prime, can you walk to infinity by stepping only to neighboring primes within distance √k?

Your idea ↔ The math: Neighborhood graph at step √k ↔ Connectivity of the Gaussian prime lattice
02 Annulus to Strip: Unrolling the Geometry

Far from the origin, an annular ring of primes is essentially flat — curvature scales as 1/R. We can “unroll” the ring into a rectangular strip where the x-axis is angle and y-axis is radius. This transforms a curved 2D problem into a flat one, which is far easier to analyze with matrix methods.

Your idea ↔ The math: Translate to (0,0) ↔ Cylinder coordinates — local coords where curvature vanishes
03 Why Fixed-Width Strips, Not Fixed-Angle Wedges

A fixed-angle wedge gets physically wider as R grows, so the local statistics change with radius — useless for comparison. A fixed physical-width strip shrinks in angle as Δθ = W/R, keeping local prime density comparable across all radii. This is the sampling geometry that makes transfer matrices work.

Your idea ↔ The math: Strip not annulus ↔ Fixed-width sampling — comparable local statistics
04 Prime Angle Gaps Are Random

Rudnick & Waxman (2019) showed that the angular gaps between consecutive Gaussian primes at large radius follow an exponential distribution — exactly like gaps between uniform random points. The left panel shows actual prime gaps; the right shows random gaps. They're statistically indistinguishable. This means primes don't cluster or repel — locally, they behave like Poisson rain.

05 The Transfer Matrix

The core computational tool. Given primes on an inner shell boundary and an outer shell boundary, the transfer matrix T encodes which inner ports can reach which outer ports within step distance √k. Dead columns (no connections) are where the walk can't continue. Composing T across many shells reveals long-range connectivity.

Your idea ↔ The math: Connectivity operator ↔ Transfer matrix — boundary-to-boundary reachability
06 The Four-Sided Tile

Each tile has four faces: Inner (I), Outer (O), Left (L), Right (R). Interior primes are eliminated via Schur complement — only boundary ports survive. The overlap collar of width √k ensures neighboring tiles share enough boundary to stitch together seamlessly. This is how we decompose the infinite strip into manageable pieces.

Your idea ↔ The math: Flow / electricity ↔ Schur complement — eliminate interior, keep boundary
07 Three Observables: Boolean, Channels, Gap-Runs

We measure connectivity at three resolutions. Boolean crossing: does any path cross this shell? Channel count: how many independent paths cross? (When this hits zero — that's the moat.) Gap-run field: where do large angular gaps persist across multiple shells? These “danger corridors” are where moats nucleate.

Your idea ↔ The math: Gap-run alarm ↔ Dangerous gap persistence — run-length across shells
08 Anderson Localization: When the Walk Dies

By analogy with wave propagation through random media: the walk's amplitude decays exponentially through shells of random prime barriers. The Lyapunov exponent γ is the decay rate. If γ > 0, the walk is localized — it dies out, meaning a moat exists at predictable radius. If γ ≤ 0, the walk is robust or critical.

Your idea ↔ The math: Measuring decay ↔ Lyapunov exponent — rate predicts WHERE the moat lives
09 Computational Cost: Why Strips Win

Brute-forcing a full annulus from radius 80M to 10B requires ~1018 primes — impossible. A single fixed-width strip (W=128) covers the same radial range with ~3.7×1010 primes, computable in hours. The transfer matrix reduces each shell to ~25 boundary ports. That's a ~107× compression.

Your idea ↔ The math: Annulus throughput ↔ Conductance / Min-cut — how much transport flows through the bottleneck
10 Rosetta Stone: Engineering ↔ Mathematics

Every engineering idea in this pipeline has a precise mathematical name. This table maps between the two vocabularies so you can read the number theory papers and the code side by side.
